speaker: interviewer
text: 5th of April, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia. Rolling, and camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So did you ever actually practice your diamond trade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I worked in '40, 1940-- '41 and after the war. Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked about three years into the trade. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before-- before the war you were working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Working, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you working much?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I mean, full-time. I've learned the whole trade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, it takes about three years to pick up the trade, and I-- and-- I was pretty-- pretty smart in it, too. Didn't pay much money, but you learn it. And-- and after the war, because there was a shortage in those days. And I worked for a while, not long. A year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. So it was because of that, that you were able to keep your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I had my diamonds [? fair, ?] and when my father was originally picked up, they asked name and address. And the polizei-- that's the Grune polizei-- they come to do it. The Germans. And I told them I'm gonna spare, and who have to look after me? And they kept the mother. They say yes, they kept her home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about your brother that stayed?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was-- he went underground. That is another story. He was picked up in Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was already gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was not gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, my brother was there. But he was-- he got out of it all of the time. Illegal, and then he went really underground in Holland. And his wife who's-- his wife is here. But there's another story, of course, and she got a big story to tell. Matter of fact, she was interviewed. But we in-- well, the date exactly, they 11th of February 1943, middle of the tonight, bang at the door. Grune polizei. My name to be picked up. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother was home. I said, go. She could escape with a little ladder, a little-- over the fence by the neighbors. They didn't look for her. She was not there. And I was married in that day, and me and my wife, we were picked up. And in Amsterdam was an old theater, which is now a museum, that was the assembling place of the-- [INAUDIBLE]. All diamond cutters were picked up. The whole lot were picked up in one go.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Several hundred, all picked up. And we were there maybe two, three hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to take anything with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. You had to take your [INAUDIBLE] shoes, and then a suitcase. Picked up together, bundled it together, and then-- and we had never anything ready. Some people have everything ready, were waiting until they were picked up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were you told when you were picked up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Picked up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, they just arrived--

speaker: interviewee
text: Diamond, all together. Nothing-- they say nothing. And we went to the assembling point, and the whole lot went to a camp in Holland, in Vught. Concentration camp Vught. A new camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you sent there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train, we arrived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the train ride like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, normal train.

speaker: interviewer
text: With seats?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, normal train, had seats. And was only an hour, hour and a half. And we went to camp, and we were reasonable-- yeah, we were not badly received there. Not compared to-- as a matter of fact, that-- that camp in-- in Holland saved my life in Auschwitz. If you come from-- say, from home, straightaway into a concentration camp, you wouldn't survive without that intermediate camp. That camp was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So, what, you mean you got used to camp life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but the way first-- a lot of people died in the camp, too. The concentration camp in Holland was a bad camp, SS camp. The same, it was a concentration camp. But you had contact with the outside world a bit. There was people, instead of to jail, they would send them to concentration camps. After three or four months, they let them go. We-- our commanders were not so hard. They picked out 10 boys immediately for assisting the newcomers. We even went to the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were one of those 10?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But I could go run away, too. I had a chance to go away. But I had my wife in camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if one left, if one escaped, they sent 20 straightaway to elsewhere. Auschwitz, most probably. And in that camp, there was a separate men and women's camp. And the center was the camp from criminals, so it was political. The kaps were bad [? with ?] Germans. But we were young, and we could-- our work was reasonable. And now, in that camp, why did they pick us up? They build a diamond factory in camp. A complete factory. You know, and diamond is cut by disk, and they're building whole factory. Took them months, and they never worked. But they built the factory. Another thing in Vught, Vught is very close to Eindhoven, and Eindhoven is Philips. Philips had a lot of Jewish people working. And apparently the Germans made an arrangement with the Philips, to build a Philips factory in camp. Let those Jewish people from Philips work in camp, which they did. They got special Philips food. Every-- there is only 10, 15 kilometers from Eindhoven from the Philips-- you know Philips is a very big factory, even in those days. And they had Philips food every day. We had part of them, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got some of that food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, our-- we were good, we had parcels, too. My uncle sent parcels, so we could live there. And you-- you-- the first three, four months was pretty within reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of work were you doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, more or less, in-- in maintenance, you know, assisting people who came in, and helped them from the train. And-- and-- and odd jobs and-- and overseeing, more or less.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would you tell the people who were arriving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Don't-- don't worry, here. It's not bad here. You can live here. You know, you-- you-- they don't kill you here. Some [? were ?] [? bad. ?] I had one experience. Once I had to work in the-- sometimes you-- you worked outside. And there was an SS man, Dutch SS. And he looked at me, he said, I know you. I said, from where? He said, you play there, the music.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had a little of respect for me. Had a big dog with him. He said, don't worry. I say, what are you doing here? He didn't know. He thought he could make some money. Extra. You know, those were those brat SS, they didn't know. Young boys, 17 or 18 years. Uniform looks good. Didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Vught, it's getting slowly worse. My mother came to the camp with my aunt. She thought she would be all right. She went voluntary with my aunt, my aunt was picked up. She went with-- she was there for very short time, and then she was sent away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so did you see her when she was in camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I've seen her every day. There was two camps, but I had-- I could go in both camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Did you have a special pass?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, we had some special-- on the arm, and band for [INAUDIBLE], yes. And-- and then they start sending. And-- an aunt of mine came with her children, and then there was a transport, all the women and children were sent away. Oh, was a few months later. They kept on going by the hundreds.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you hear when-- when a transport was about to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, knew nothing. But I hear now, yes, about-- we knew nothing. I know they went to Germany, they went to-- to Poland or where, but they knew nothing. Even the-- we spoke to the SS sometimes, you know, you could sometimes speak to-- to and ask. They never knew anything, or didn't want to . Or they didn't know. They were not from there, and the communication was different. They were very smart in Holland, the SS. They didn't send them from Auschwitz to Holland. You know, they-- they didn't know, because one knows-- one camp doesn't know from the other camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have anybody special there that you could-- like that SS guy that recognized you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was only one, and very brief, no. No. No. We had never contact with anyone, no. The SS were-- I tell you some very-- very odd story in camp. Amongst all the diamond cutters was one fellow, he was like an Adonis. He was in-- in-- a film star, he really was. And a good friend of mine, tall and good looking. And-- and he was picked up by an Aufseherin. That is a German-- a German SS, Aufseherin. And he have to be the boy from-- from her. She fell in love with him. He didn't want to, of course, but he had a good life with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the cleaner in her-- in her apartment, or whatever it was. I remember that story. Oh, yes, a lot of those things happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so were you playing music there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We had-- yeah. We had-- I had no instrument with me, because they disappeared. But we could play other instruments, and we-- we played, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: All over the place. People wrote, they asked for, they-- they-- the instruments were there. And yes, we had-- matter of fact, we had a little band with Martin Roman-- I'll never forget him. German. And yes, we had--

speaker: interviewer
text: So a German-- a German Jew? Or a German--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jew. All the Germans were the-- the Germans there and the Nazis came, and they were sitting there, and then had entertainment. And-- and-- Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were-- sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you treated differently after the concerts?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Not-- not-- no, not over there, there's no difference. It was after hours, after time. But like I say, it-- it-- Vught was-- you could survive. It was too much in camp, what can I say? Too much in Holland. There was communication with the outside world, you see? They-- they-- they-- we maybe thought all camps are like that. They're working there, and then-- then you got just enough to eat, and you survive. It was not like that. One day they started transports, and-- and one day-- everything was overnight. In the morning, all the women were lined up-- like my young wife-- and transport. Only women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get to say goodbye?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were gone. Middle of the night, they were picked up, empty all the barracks, on appell and gone. In the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we seen it. We knew it. Appellplatz, we knew it. Couldn't get near it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. Terrible. And they-- and that's what they did all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what sorts of things like when you-- did you know your wife would be--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you can't describe it, it-- it was terrible. I mean, the young wife, 21 years. And not me, all the other ones. We didn't know where they went to. No idea. They started rolling up, rolling up, worse and worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did your attitude change when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very hard. And it--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you mean hard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you-- you get selfish. It's-- it's a matter of survival, you know, to-- Like, my mother was there, and she was gone. Then my aunt was there. Everyone was disappearing. But you get hard in camp, like I say. In-- in that camp, you learned the-- the-- the trade, to be tough. Because we had a good life at home. Many other countries had a hard life at home. The difference was too much. But there, you get-- believe in the discipline. And the kapos were very bad, you know, the German-- all German kapos. And all were ex-convicts that came out of the-- out of jails. I had one kapo, an old fellow, he was in jail since 1924. He was pretty old. He killed his whole family in Germany. So those kind of people, they sent and they were our kapos. Mad, mad men, mad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so, did you see your mother before-- when was the last time you saw your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also gone. So end, gone. Overnight. Couldn't say goodbye, nothing. Just gone. We knew they were gone, but you couldn't get near them. We had a big camp, I mean, there were several thousand people there. They were so organized, the Germans were-- Everything was done very slowly, slowly. And I had the Judenrat, you know, the-- the-- the Jewish organization. The rabbi and then the business people, and they-- and they had meetings all the time. And they tied the screw more and more and more. All the time, more and more. And-- and you get used to it. And at the end, you know, you're-- you're nothing anymore. Nobody. And in Vught, work-- yes, they had work in Philips.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never. I worked in Vught, and then-- well, they shot a lot of planes down. And all those planes, they came to there. German planes, American planes. And all those wrecks, they came to camp. And we have to take them apart. And they told us, that part is good, and that part. And if knew one part that was very valuable, we just put a hammer on it, and see there was nothing left of it. You know, we took it apart, iron by iron and-- and-- and that's it. That's what I did for a while, yeah. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you leave there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Vught, end of the year. It was quite a while-- nine, 10 months. I don't know the dates. I would say I was in Vught November or December, '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was about--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then suddenly, we went-- also in-- also unexpected in appell. We rode them-- Westerbork. Westerbork is the [GERMAN]. We went Vught to Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you told anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And in Westerbork it was, maybe, overnight?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get to Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Train. Normal train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Travelled the same way again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, normal trains. In Westerbork, overnight, and then went a goods train, straight off Auschwitz. We didn't know. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what was it-- what did it feel like, when you went on a goods train after traveling there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know. No idea. Couldn't think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any discussion among people [? in there? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Our anger-- frightened. Young, young-- we were all very young. 22, 23-- young people. Oh, we said old people. You know, we had a lot of old people amongst us. That is bad. With young people, they thought, oh, we-- we-- we're young. We're strong. You see, in-- in-- I would say in the Vught in camp, we was pretty well fed. Not well fed-- not well fed-- but you could live when you're young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course, a lot of people died there, too. Old people. The luck that I have, I would say in camp is no diseases. If you got, sick you're gone. You were gone. And in-- in Vught, a lot of people died there, too. But not from-- from hunger, but from-- from [INAUDIBLE] from the work, but from diseases, you know? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you still had hope when you were on the train, did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Do we have hope? That comes later. You see, when-- I don't know how long the-- the-- from-- I got-- I got no story in Westerbork, because it was only overnight. You came there, and the next day-- Westerbork was a camp where people-- That was virtually the main [GERMAN], what they call from Holland, what they call, from Westerbork. Not Vught, Vught was a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: With Westerbork was in-- in-- I'm going to say, a lot station. And from there, everything went via Westerbork to Auschwitz. There's one thing I can't understand. And I ask the question many times, still. I don't know how many rail-- the-- the railway station in Westerbork was [INAUDIBLE], was a little station. Didn't the Allied know that those people went from there to Germany? Why didn't they bomb the railway line? That is a big question. Never, never ever. And why did the underground, the Dutch underground, blow it up? They're the questions we ask today, still. Nothing happened. Were you expecting on those train rides to be-- that the underground might be there? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't know. But then, you see, what happened to us between, say, Westerbork and on the way to Auschwitz. And then, so fast. You see, we were in Vught one day, straight on to Westerbork and immediately sent through to Auschwitz to camp. Auschwitz to-- to-- to Germany. And then when we arrived, then the-- then the big story starts. We arrived in-- I don't know how long the trip was. Two days. Or-- or-- one or two days. Or three days, two nights.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have anything to eat on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us something to eat on the train in Westerbork, yes. They gave us food for a few days. And had in the corner even, you had-- a little like cubicle toilets there, which we had then. [INAUDIBLE]. And well, I may arrive in Auschwitz in Birkenau. Well, that was it. Then you start suddenly realizing, we were in hell.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like? Did you arrive at night or during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nighttime. Late, it was, you know, nighttime, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the doors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Doors open there, and the SS straightaway hitting and doing and-- and-- and shouting. And a lot of children. And-- and-- and then separate. Where you going to still go? And then they start selecting. We didn't know. Left-- all the men. Right-- the women. Children. It's mothers and children straightaway in the other side. Trucks standing there, and-- and they start selecting, which we didn't know. Left, left, right, left, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Easy. I was young. Straightaway, by the-- you see, when you're 22, you were straightaway by the-- the work. We didn't have worries. But people from 40, 45, then they hesitate, some of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a feeling at all, what the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No idea. Well, an hour later, we knew. Then we knew. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after you were selected?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're standing there, and then marching into, well, [GERMAN], they call it. All your clothes off. Shaven all your hairs off. Number tattooed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here is my number. Still there. Very good quality after so many years. [LAUGHS] And-- and then we start-- and then you speak to the-- to the-- well, everything is done by the-- by the-- the [INAUDIBLE], you know, the-- the-- how do you call it? The camp, the Jewish camp who would work. They did all the work-- the doctors, everything was Jewish. Well, mainly Polish. And they start telling you, oh, you got six-- six weeks to live. Then we knew. [GERMAN] is here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they say?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN]. That's where we're going. Up there. And then the smell, already it start immediately. As soon as those transports arrived, they went straight on to the-- to the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like when you realized that smell and what--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Unbelievable. It-- it-- it's so fast, you don't know. You don't. You don't. You can't. You can't even-- even comprehend it. It all went that fast. You don't sleep. You got no idea. Suddenly, you get all your clothes gone. You've got your striped clothing on. You're nobody. And you're a number. You don't know. Frightened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And those-- there were people were gone in-- in-- in-- in a few days. They couldn't. They-- they-- they-- There's no [? description ?] for it. And then I-- I was in Birkenau-- you've heard the name Birkenau-- only a few days, and was selected. And-- and all those diamonds, we were together all the time, and then they were split up all over the place. And they pick me for a camp in Monowitz. You've heard of Monowitz? It's Buna. IG Farben work. And I worked at IG Farben. [INAUDIBLE]. Big camp, and I was very fortunate. The next day, they were hanging six men. Immediately. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six-- that's normal there. You know, they've done escaped or something, and we were marching around to see them hanging those, while entering camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the first time you'd seen anybody hung?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The second day there, one night I was there. As it happened, they made an odd ceremony, the marching by. And then I came in a kommando there. And I worked in the Strassenbau. I was making streets outside. It's not so bad, outside kommando, because [INAUDIBLE] was artificial rubber works, and I got-- and I worked in a street kommando. And I went to the music-- this music barrack there, and I met some people there who I knew. And they told me, your brother is here. He was in-- he played the piano. He was in the-- in the band. I couldn't go into music. I didn't have no instruments, and it was full. Someone had to die first before they got a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there's actually a barracks for--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It's not a separate barracks, they had a normal kommando, but they could rehearse together. So I ask one fellow I remember well, Louis Van Der Molan, who I-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name, sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Louis Van Der Molan. And I knew very well. He said, your brother was-- I was looking for my father. They said-- They say, your brother was here. My brother was picked up in Belgium-- I didn't even know that he was picked up-- and finished up in that camp six months before me. And he was in-- in-- in hospital, in the Krankenbau, he was sick. I found him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like when you saw him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was very excitable, yeah, very-- He was already very far gone. And he survived the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he have?

speaker: interviewee
text: What does he  have? A sickness. What do-- what do you have? A sickness. You don't know. I don't-- I still got marks on my legs, and I don't know what you got for sickness. You die there. As long as you don't have typhus. Typhus was the one that died you, and-- and cholera and all those diseases. Filth, it was all filth. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, sorry, you saw your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, it was a few days later. He worked somewhere else. And-- yeah, we were together, in [? other ?] kommando, but he-- he-- he was quite capable. And he could play the piano, and he play the piano accordion.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I'm sorry. When you first saw him, he was in hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, after he came out. He came out, in hospital I couldn't get near him. I saw him in hospital, I smuggled in. I start doing like a gardener, take a rake and start cleaning up and shouting everywhere. And then I got him. And he was-- he survived, more or less there. And, yeah, I was always nervous, of course, often. Nighttime. You know, we came together-- Dutch by Dutch, Hungarians by Hungarians, and Greeks by Greeks. All had their own little groups.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so it was-- there wasn't much mixing outside of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Very little [? and ?] work. Very little [? and ?] work. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the other kommando. [INAUDIBLE] And I got later and better kommando, because-- when-- I talk about periods. I can tell from camp quite a few things that are from periods. In-- in-- in Buna, Monowitz, then I could have an instrument. I didn't play in the marching out band, because they was full, but certainly we played amongst themselves. We had to play for the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, oh, yes, they made evenings and we could play for them. Modern American music, they loved it. Oh, yes. Or German, you know, we'd play. And-- and they had-- in camp was in-- was in [? Puff. ?] You've heard about that too? There were prostitutes in camp. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were mainly Polish girls. They made them-- I don't know if there were Jewish girls amongst them, I can't tell you. And-- and they entertained them, but non-Jews. Say, Polish prisoners, if they worked well. They're getting certificates. And when they had enough, four or five, they could go to the [? Puff. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never heard that?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, what did you call them?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Puff. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Puff? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was. And-- and we called it a [? Puff. ?] It was like a-- a brothel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the-- not for the Jews. Only for the Polish prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And where the girls came from, I don't know. I never had contact with any of them. Seen them, when the Germans entertained them. You know, they-- healthy, young, but who they were, I don't know. I don't know what happened to them. Maybe gypsies, there were a lot of gypsies in camp, too. You see, we had such a mixture of concentration camp people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape two of the interview with Samuel Lissing. It's the 5th of April, 1995, and we are in Sydney, Australia.
